title: Regular Expressions 🔍
description: Master the dark art of Regex to instantly search and destroy strings.

slides.title: The Search Problem 🔍
slides.content:
Imagine you have a giant string: `"My email is john@gmail.com!"`.

How do you mathematically extract JUST the email address? You can't just use `.include?("@")` because that only tells you IF it exists, not WHERE it is or WHAT the full address is.

To perform powerful pattern matching, you need Regular Expressions (Regex).

slides.title: The Regex Slashes `//`
slides.content:
Just like Strings are defined using quotes `""`, Regular Expressions are defined using forward slashes `//`.

The characters you put perfectly inside the slashes define the strict visual pattern Ruby will furiously search for.

slides.title: The `=~` Match Operator
slides.content: Run this! We use the `=~` operator to ask the string if it perfectly contains the Regex pattern. It returns the exact numerical Index of where the pattern magically starts!
slides.codeSnippet:
sentence = "My code is completely broken!"

# Look for exactly 'code'
location = sentence =~ /code/

puts "The pattern starts exactly at index: #{location}"

# If it fails, it returns nil
puts sentence =~ /wizard/

quizData.question: Which specific symbols are strictly used in Ruby to beautifully officially create a Regular Expression pattern object?
quizData.explanation: Slapping slashes gracefully around text instantly turns it into a highly optimized search pattern object.

slides.title: Regex Magic Symbols ✨
slides.content:
Regex isn't just for exact words. You use magic characters to build flexible patterns!

- `\d` matches exactly ANY single digit (0-9).
- `\w` matches exactly ANY single letter or number.
- `+` means 'one or more' of the previous thing.

slides.title: Advanced Extraction
slides.content: Run this! We use `.match` to elegantly perfectly extract a continuous block of numbers regardless of what those exact numbers actually are!
slides.codeSnippet:
data = "Order ID: 89452 processed."

# \d means digit. + means one or more.
# So \d+ means 'give me a continuous block of numbers'
result = data.match(/\d+/)

puts "Extracted ID: #{result}"

quizData.question: In the brilliant dark art of Regex, what exactly does the magic `\d` explicitly elegantly search for?
quizData.explanation: Regex `\d` intelligently represents definitively any number 0 through 9.
